original_abstract: Evaluating the similarity between various units of text—from individual words to entire documents—is a foundational requirement for numerous Natural Language Processing (NLP) applications. These include information retrieval, document clustering, machine translation, and automated grading systems for essays and short answers. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of existing text similarity research by categorizing methods into three primary frameworks: String-based, Corpus-based, and Knowledge-based approaches. String-based measures focus on lexical patterns and character sequences, while Corpus-based and Knowledge-based techniques leverage large-scale datasets and semantic networks to capture deeper meaning. Additionally, this paper explores the growing trend of hybrid models that integrate multiple similarity metrics to achieve higher performance and more robust results.
humanized_abstract: Text similarity detection is a prerequisite for a large number of Natural Language Processing (NLP) applications. Such applications include information retrieval, document clustering, machine translation, and in particular, automated assessment of student responses to open-ended essay and short answer questions. This survey surveys existing research on text similarity detection and organizes them into three mainstream frameworks: String-based, Corpus-based, and Knowledge-based. String-based approaches focus on the lexical and spatial patterns of characters, while the other two approaches explore lexical, syntactic and semantic patterns in corpora and knowledge bases. Hybrid approaches are also emerging as combinations of different approaches are often found to improve performances and robustness of similarity assessment systems.
